summaries.Akify:
Akify   (internal + external)

What lands here  
All mail that references the Akify company or workspace: Slack or Linear alerts, Firebase sign-in notices, calendar invitations, NDA requests, investor intros, and follow-up threads about fundraising, decks, or partnerships.

Typical sender intent  
• Internal tools (Slack, Linear, Firebase) – notify me of mentions, tasks, or security sign-ins.  
• Investors/partners – request meetings, ask for materials, share feedback, or send legal docs (NDAs).  
• Teammates – coordinate schedules or circulate updated collateral.

Triage & response  
1. Security or sign-in alerts: confirm legitimacy, then archive.  
2. Calendar invites: accept/decline within 4 h so slots stay current.  
3. Slack/Linear digests: skim for urgent blockers, otherwise mark read—primary action happens in the app.  
4. Investor or partner threads: reply same day, thank them, address questions, attach latest deck or link, and propose next step. Flag in CRM.  
5. Legal docs: forward to co-founder + counsel, sign via DocuSign, return within 48 h.

Style & policies  
Keep replies concise, solution-oriented, and free of confidential customer data. Always bcc co-founder on external outreach to maintain visibility.
summaries.Other:
Other – Miscellaneous & low-structure traffic (120–180 wds)

What lands here  
• Messages that don’t cleanly match a project, teaching, grant, or admin label: ad-hoc coordination with colleagues, informal advice threads, one-off scheduling, forwarded seminar notices, purchase/IT receipts, security alerts, recruiting spam, and cold outreach.

Sender intent / common asks  
• Quick clarifications (“bring 10 poster boards”), slide feedback, “are you free?” scheduling, RSVP confirmations, automated status (incident opened/closed, OTP, calendly booking), marketing or job ads. Urgency varies from minutes (OTP) to ignorable (job blast).

Triage & response strategy  
1. Scan subject line for time-sensitive tokens: OTP, incident, “urgent,” calendar accept/decline. Act within the same session.  
2. Scheduling: reply with availability or confirm invite; then move to Calendar or the dedicated “Meetings” folder.  
3. Collegial FYIs / advice: respond briefly or acknowledge; archive.  
4. Marketing/job spam: delete or unsubscribe.  
5. Receipts & order confirmations: save to “Purchases” subfolder for bookkeeping.

Style & policies  
• Keep replies concise, friendly, and decisive; include action owner and deadline when relevant.  
• Never forward security tokens; delete once used.  
• When in doubt, re-file to a more specific category to reduce future noise.
summaries.DAPLab:
DAPLab category = all correspondence tied to the lab’s workshops, partner program, and event operations. Typical messages include • workshop/retreat invitations, calendar updates, Zoom links • partner, faculty, or student follow-ups after meetings • logistical questions (building access, registration, slides, dinner RSVPs) • expense and poster-printing reimbursements routed through the CS ticket system • slack/zoom auto-notifications related to DAPLab events.

Sender intent is usually to: confirm attendance, adjust meeting times, request location or security details, share notes or slides, claim small reimbursements, or explore/expand an industry partnership.

Triage & response: 
1. Time-sensitive items (location changes, security lists, schedule polls) → reply within a few hours, copying the core organizers list (Lydia, Eugene, Dmytro).  
2. Reimbursement acknowledgments from tickets.cs can be archived—no action unless documents are missing.  
3. Partnership or speaker inquiries → propose slots, attach the latest “Partner FAQ” deck, and loop in the faculty lead.  
4. General thanks/notes → a brief courtesy reply, then file.

Style & policy: speak as “we/the lab,” keep a collegial tone, avoid sharing confidential partner material publicly, and log all accepted commitments in the shared workshop tracker.
summaries.Humor Project:
Humor Project contains all traffic tied to “The Humor Project™” research/course. Typical threads include:  
• students asking to enroll late, adjust or defer course credits, obtain elective approvals, or request recommendation letters  
• logistics (meeting times, room bookings, Google Drive/Slack/GitHub/Supabase invites, Linear/Jira updates) and quick technical fixes (integration approvals, quota issues) raised by team members  
• automated alerts from GitHub, Slack, Linear, and other Dev Ops tools requiring owner authorization

Sender intent is usually to secure academic sign-off, schedule time with me, gain access, or flag a task/blocker. Most requests are time-sensitive and actionable by me as PI/instructor.

Triage strategy:  
1. Student academic admin (credits, registration, rec letters) – reply within 24 h; copy relevant program office if needed.  
2. Meeting scheduling – propose slots or loop assistant, then archive.  
3. Access/integration approvals – verify requester is on project roster, approve or reject, then archive notification.  
4. Automated status pings – skim for blockers, forward to dev lead, mark read.

Use brief, encouraging tone; confirm next steps and include links/forms where possible.
summaries.DoubleAgents:
DoubleAgents folder = everything tied to the “DoubleAgents” research project and lab group.

Typical content  
• Internal project traffic: calendar invites/acceptances, “running late” or “heading up” messages, weekly-progress notes, draft papers, slides, demo links, poster logistics, CHI submission results.  
• External interest: students or collaborators asking to join the project, request supervision, letters of rec, or copies of slides/talks.

Sender intent  
• Coordinate meetings and deadlines.  
• Share or request project artifacts (docs, Overleaf, Drive, Zoom links).  
• Seek feedback, travel approvals, recommendation letters, or research opportunities.

Triage & response  
1. Scheduling/RSVP → confirm or propose alternate slot; keep calendar updated.  
2. Document/share requests → send file or Drive link; file a copy in the shared project folder.  
3. Prospective students → brief acknowledgement within 48 h; forward to lab-interest sheet or invite to office hours if promising.  
4. Recommendation letters → verify deadline & materials, add to rec-tracker.  
5. Low-priority FYI (e.g., “yay we submitted”) → archive after a quick emoji or congrats.

Key policies  
• Treat unpublished manuscripts/demos as confidential.  
• Follow Columbia rec-letter policy (require CV + statement).  
• Maintain supportive, concise tone; default to group visibility unless PII or sensitive.
summaries.Jumpstarter:
Jumpstarter  

Scope: All messages tied to the “JumpStarter” research project—collaborator chatter, student inquiries to join, calendar invites, Overleaf shares, and automated notifications from OpenReview, AAAI, IUI, EMNLP, etc.  

Typical sender intent  
• Co-authors: coordinate experiments, drafts, rebuttals, conference track choices, or set up Zoom/Meet calls.  
• Conference systems: deliver status updates (submission, reviews, decisions).  
• Students/mentees: request to contribute or seek guidance.  

Triage / response  
1. Scan for hard deadlines (rebuttal windows, camera-ready, meeting times). Add to task list and calendar immediately.  
2. For co-author requests, reply within 24 h; confirm next steps or supply materials (figures, text, repo links).  
3. File system notifications and citations in the project folder for record-keeping; no reply needed unless action indicated.  
4. For new-student inquiries, send template clarifying current openings and required commitment.  

Key policies & style  
• Maintain submission confidentiality and double-blind rules; discuss reviewer comments only within the author team.  
• Use concise, decision-oriented replies; include checklist of who does what by when.  
• Cc the full author list when decisions affect the manuscript or timeline.
